why_correct: Temperature=0.0 engages greedy decoding — the model always selects the maximum-probability token at each step, collapsing the sampling distribution to a deterministic argmax. For deterministic business logic like routing rules, you want zero stochastic variance in the generation path; the same input must always produce the same output branch. This is the canonical 'code/logic generation' pattern: whenever correctness is binary and reproducibility is a hard requirement, temperature=0.0 is not a preference, it's an architectural constraint.

distractors.B: The retry-match strategy is the 'poll until consensus' anti-pattern — it looks like a safety net but it's actually a latency multiplier with undefined worst-case cost. At temperature=0.5 you've reduced variance but not eliminated it, so you're now paying 2-N API calls per request to probabilistically converge on an answer you could have gotten deterministically on the first call. In production this breaks under load: tail latency spikes and cost scales unpredictably. The fix is upstream, not compensatory.
distractors.C: max_tokens controls the output budget, not the reasoning process or output stability. Conflating token budget with output determinism is a common misconception — more tokens gives the model room to generate more text, but if temperature is non-zero, that additional generation surface introduces more variance, not less. In production, increasing max_tokens here would make the misrouting problem worse, not better.
distractors.D: System prompt placement affects instruction priority and injection attack surface, but it has no effect on sampling temperature. Output variance is a function of the decoding strategy, not message role. Routing logic in the system prompt is good practice for trust boundary reasons, but it does nothing to stabilize outputs across calls when temperature is non-zero.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for this class of problem: if the output is executable logic, a routing decision, or any other case where two different valid-looking outputs produce different real-world outcomes, temperature=0.0 is the architectural default. Reserve non-zero temperature for tasks where output diversity is a feature, not a bug — creative generation, brainstorming, synthetic data. The question 'do I want the same input to always produce the same output?' is the deciding gate.
cross_domain_note: This intersects directly with agentic loop design: any tool-use or function-calling step that gates downstream actions should also pin temperature=0.0, since non-deterministic tool selection in an agentic chain compounds variance across steps and makes the system nearly impossible to debug or audit.

why_correct: Temperature directly controls the width of the sampling distribution — at 0.7, the model draws from a meaningfully broader probability mass than the argmax-adjacent behavior you want for deterministic structured extraction. A shared hardcoded temperature is an architectural violation of the principle that inference parameters are task-scoped contracts, not service-level constants. The correct pattern here is to expose temperature as a per-call or per-endpoint parameter with validated, role-appropriate defaults: ~0.0–0.2 for extraction, 0.6–0.8 for conversational agents. This preserves the shared wrapper's operational benefits while respecting the fundamentally different accuracy/creativity tradeoffs each consumer has.

distractors.A: Prompt constraints reduce the model's output space at the semantic level, but temperature operates at the token sampling level — you're fighting a sampling misconfiguration with instruction engineering. This might suppress some surface-level format errors but doesn't eliminate the root cause: the model is still sampling from a wider distribution and can hallucinate plausible-but-wrong field values that pass your output schema checks. A is the right lever when the problem is instruction ambiguity; this problem is parameter misconfiguration.
distractors.C: This is the 'bigger model fixes everything' misconception. Larger models shift the probability distribution toward higher-quality tokens, but temperature still controls how broadly you sample from whatever distribution you're in. A misconfigured temperature on a larger model still produces high-variance outputs for extraction tasks — you've paid more per token and moved the hallucination rate only marginally. Model tier is a lever for capability ceiling, not sampling discipline.
distractors.D: Post-processing retry loops treat a configuration error as a runtime error and compound the cost — you're now paying for multiple failed inference calls instead of fixing the upstream cause. In production this breaks under load: intermittent hallucinations at high temperature can produce structurally valid but semantically wrong JSON that passes schema validation and never triggers a retry, so your SLA degrades silently.

mental_model: Any time a shared inference wrapper serves consumers with divergent output-type requirements — structured vs. generative — hardcoded sampling parameters are a design smell. The CCA heuristic: inference parameters are caller contracts, not service defaults. Centralized wrappers should enforce valid ranges and safe defaults, not impose a single value across heterogeneous workloads.
cross_domain_note: This is the same boundary-of-responsibility pattern that appears in multi-tenant agentic systems: a shared orchestration layer must be configurable per-agent rather than enforcing uniform behavior, or you get tool-use and context-handling mismatches across agent types running on the same loop.

why_correct: The Anthropic client handles auth, connection pooling, and retry logic — none of which are temperature-sensitive. Temperature is a per-request inference parameter, not a client-level concern, so it belongs at call time in messages.create(), not at instantiation. B correctly separates the connection lifecycle from the inference configuration, letting the shared client remain a singleton while each caller drives its own sampling behavior. Validated per-task defaults at the wrapper layer also give you a sensible fallback without coupling callers to internal model defaults.

distractors.A: The failure mode is over-indexing on 'separate config requires separate client.' The Anthropic client carries no temperature state — instantiating one per team buys you nothing except wasted connections and duplicated retry/auth logic. A would be correct if the divergence were in auth credentials or base URL, where client-level config actually matters.
distractors.C: This is a classic shared-mutable-state anti-pattern dressed up as config management. Environment variables are process-global; reloading them per-request doesn't make them per-caller — it creates a race condition the moment two requests are in flight simultaneously. In production this breaks under any concurrency.
distractors.D: Forcing required parameters with no defaults imposes caller burden and breaks backward compatibility every time you onboard a new consumer. It also pushes validation responsibility to every callsite rather than centralizing it in the wrapper, which is the entire value of having a shared service.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: client instantiation owns connection concerns, messages.create() owns inference concerns. Any parameter that legitimately varies per request — temperature, max_tokens, system prompt — belongs at call time, not at client construction. If the shared client already handles auth and pooling correctly, don't touch it; add a thin parameterization layer in the request handler.
cross_domain_note: This same boundary — what belongs in client init versus request payload — reappears in prompt caching design: cache-eligible prefixes are set at the message level, not the client level, for the same reason that inference behavior is request-scoped.

why_correct: The root cause here isn't temperature variance — it's that ambiguous inputs are reaching irreversible tool calls at all. B inserts a confidence gate before the decision boundary, routing low-confidence cases to human review rather than trying to tune sampling parameters to compensate for genuine semantic ambiguity in the input. When mutations are irreversible and the review window is hours away, the correct pattern is to shrink the blast radius by deferring uncertain decisions, not to optimize the sampler. This is a trust boundary + agentic loop design fix, not a temperature fix.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is conflating determinism with correctness. Temperature 0.0 makes the subagent pick the highest-logit token consistently, but if the input is genuinely ambiguous, the highest-confidence output is still wrong — just reproducibly wrong. A would have deleted those three accounts on every run rather than probabilistically. A is the right move when you need reproducible routing on well-defined inputs; it doesn't help when the input itself is underspecified.
distractors.C: This is the 'dial it down a bit' anti-pattern — it looks like a calibrated response but produces no architectural improvement. 0.2 still routes ambiguous inputs to a tool call; it just does so with slightly less variance. In production, C still deletes accounts on ambiguous requests, just less often. The misconception is that temperature controls decision quality; it controls sampling distribution. Neither 0.2 nor 0.0 addresses the missing human-in-the-loop for low-confidence, high-stakes branches.
distractors.D: Majority vote at temperature 0.5 averages out noise but amplifies the underlying ambiguity — if two of three draws land on permanent_delete for an ambiguous input, you've now tripled your token cost and tripled your latency to reach the same wrong answer with higher apparent confidence. This also triples the cost per routing decision with no improvement to correctness on genuinely ambiguous inputs.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for irreversible agentic tool calls: temperature is a sampling knob, not a safety mechanism. Any time a pipeline combines (1) irreversible side effects + (2) no automated rollback + (3) unattended execution, the architecture must include an explicit confidence threshold that routes uncertain cases out of the automated path — full stop. Tune temperature only after that gate exists.
cross_domain_note: This pattern maps directly to the safety and human oversight domain: the 'minimal footprint' principle for agentic systems states that irreversible or high-impact actions should require elevated confirmation. A confidence-routing queue is the operational implementation of that principle in a batch pipeline context.

why_correct: Temperature is a per-inference sampling parameter resolved at the model server during token generation — client instantiation is irrelevant to that mechanism. Re-instantiating the SDK client creates a new HTTP connection wrapper, not a new sampling context; each call independently samples from the probability distribution shaped by temperature. At 0.6, the token distribution for marginally ambiguous classification boundaries is wide enough to produce non-deterministic tool selection, and 8% misrouting on ambiguous records is exactly the expected failure signature. The correct pattern for irreversible tool dispatch in an agentic loop is always temperature 0.0 — you're not looking for creative routing, you're looking for deterministic selection.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is conflating client state with model sampling state. The SDK client holds no temperature memory between calls — but that's not the argument that saves you. Temperature 0.6 produces non-deterministic sampling on every single call independently, and stateless calls at 0.6 still each carry the same per-call variance. This reasoning would only be valid if temperature were some kind of cumulative drift that resets, which it isn't.
distractors.C: This is the 'infrastructure smell' misdirection — it attributes a statistical sampling artifact to a wiring problem that doesn't exist. Per-stage client instantiation doesn't introduce prompt drift; the system prompt and tool definitions are passed per-call regardless of client reuse. Consolidating clients changes nothing about temperature's effect on token sampling and leaves the 8% misroute rate untouched.
distractors.D: Tool descriptions constrain the output format, not the probability mass over valid options. When two tools are both syntactically valid resolutions for an ambiguous input, temperature determines which one wins — tool schemas provide no tie-breaking determinism. This is the near-miss because it sounds like architectural reasoning, but it conflates output-space constraint with sampling determinism.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for agentic tool selection with irreversible side effects: temperature 0.0 is mandatory, full stop. Any non-zero temperature means you are probabilistically sampling across valid tool candidates on every call — tool descriptions define the candidate set, they don't collapse it to a single deterministic choice. Client topology is a red herring whenever the symptom is a statistical error rate.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary and cost-of-error domain: irreversible database mutations are exactly the class of side effects where the agentic loop design must eliminate variance at the dispatch point, not absorb it downstream with retry logic or compensating transactions.

why_correct: Temperature directly controls the sharpness of the token probability distribution at sampling time — at 0.0, you're doing argmax sampling, so the model always picks the single highest-probability token, collapsing variance entirely. For deterministic schema enforcement, that's the correct pattern: when the schema is fully specified in the system prompt, the 'correct' field name already sits at high probability, and you need the sampler to stop competing tokens from occasionally winning. The inconsistency the team is observing is exactly the failure mode of a non-zero temperature on a constrained-output task.

distractors.A: The misconception is that temperature acts as a 'parser flexibility' dial. It doesn't — temperature affects sampling randomness, not the model's ability to interpret varied input formats. Setting 0.5 just means 'customerId' and 'cust_id' still win occasionally instead of frequently. Input format variety is handled by few-shot examples or richer prompt instructions, not by relaxing output temperature.
distractors.C: This is the normalize-downstream anti-pattern — it looks workable until you're operating at scale with dozens of field names and schema versions, at which point the post-processing layer becomes a second schema maintenance burden that drifts from the source of truth. More directly: increasing temperature to 1.0 actively amplifies the variance you're trying to eliminate. Post-processing is a compensating control, not a substitute for fixing the root cause at the sampler.
distractors.D: Tiering temperature by ticket priority conflates input routing logic with output consistency requirements. The schema contract is identical for all tickets — 'customer_id' should always be 'customer_id' regardless of ticket priority. This adds operational complexity with zero architectural benefit.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: when the task has a single correct output shape — structured JSON, fixed schema, canonical field names — temperature goes to 0.0, full stop. Temperature > 0 is only justified when output diversity is a feature, not a bug. If you're fighting output variance on a constrained-format task, the sampler is always the first lever to check.
cross_domain_note: This connects directly to the reliability domain: in agentic tool-use loops where downstream steps depend on consistent JSON keys, temperature > 0 on extraction steps creates cascading parse failures that are hard to attribute without tracing back to the sampler configuration.

why_correct: Temperature is a per-call parameter, not a conversation-level constant — the correct pattern is to treat each turn's inference goal independently and set temperature accordingly. Requirement-gathering turns are constraint-satisfaction tasks where schema compliance and factual fidelity demand low entropy; generation turns are creative synthesis tasks where diversity in the output distribution is the actual product requirement. Locking temperature at 0.1 globally collapses the output distribution during generation, causing the model to exploit the highest-probability phrasing regardless of how distinct the client inputs are. Phase-varying temperature is the canonical architectural response when a single conversation contains turns with fundamentally different fidelity-vs-diversity tradeoffs.

distractors.A: Raising temperature globally trades one problem for another — you get more varied scripts but now your JSON schema enforcement during data-collection turns is exposed to the same entropy you're trying to introduce. At 0.7, structured output validation becomes load-bearing infrastructure rather than a safety net, and you'll see malformed schema responses under real traffic volume. The fix is surgical, not global.
distractors.C: This is the 'more instructions cure everything' misconception. Detailed stylistic guidance helps the model understand what variation to produce, but if temperature is still 0.1, the model's sampling distribution is still heavily peaked — it will collapse those rich instructions into the single most probable realization of them. In production, this looks like slightly better but still repetitive output, because you've changed the target without changing the sampling behavior.
distractors.D: Model tier does not linearly correlate with output diversity at equivalent temperature settings. A larger model at 0.1 is still a low-entropy sampler — it just has a more capable peaked distribution. This also introduces cost-per-token regression and latency SLA risk with no architectural justification.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: when a multi-turn flow contains both constraint-satisfaction turns and generative turns, temperature is a turn-level parameter, not a session-level constant. Ask 'what is the cost of a wrong token here?' — if correctness is the cost, temperature goes down; if sameness is the cost, temperature goes up.
cross_domain_note: This phase-based parameterization pattern maps directly to agentic loop design — tool-call turns (structured, deterministic) and synthesis turns (open-ended) should similarly use differentiated sampling strategies when you control the orchestration layer.

why_correct: Temperature is not a pipeline-level constant — it's a per-call parameter that should be tuned to the output contract of each turn. Extraction turns have a single correct output and need near-zero temperature to avoid stochastic field corruption; the narrative turn has an open creative space where higher temperature drives lexical and structural diversity. Parameterizing the chat function preserves the single-function constraint while exposing the control surface the caller needs. This is the canonical pattern: lock temperature down where correctness is binary, open it up where variation is the goal.

distractors.A: Reducing to 0.0 globally fixes the extraction reliability problem that already wasn't broken — the retry loop handles that. At 0.0, the narrative turn becomes maximally deterministic, which is precisely the boilerplate complaint you're trying to resolve. This is the 'tighten everything' anti-pattern: it looks safe until you realize you've sacrificed the only output that needed variance.
distractors.C: System prompt expansion addresses what Claude is instructed to do, not the sampling diversity of how it does it. With temperature still frozen low, Claude will follow the new stylistic instructions but collapse to the same high-probability token sequence every time. Prompt-driven style guidance and temperature-driven sampling diversity are orthogonal levers — conflating them is the failure mode here.
distractors.D: Model tier affects capability ceiling and latency/cost, not sampling entropy. A larger model at the same low temperature will produce a more capable but equally deterministic output — still boilerplate, just better-written boilerplate. Model selection doesn't substitute for temperature control.

mental_model: When a multi-turn pipeline mixes structured extraction with open-ended generation, treat temperature as a per-turn parameter, not a pipeline constant. The architectural tell is any function that accepts a single temperature and fans it out unchanged across turns with different output contracts — that's the coupling point to break.
cross_domain_note: This same parameterization pattern applies in agentic loops: tool-call turns that parse structured JSON responses should run at low temperature while synthesis or reasoning turns benefit from higher values — hardcoding one value is a common orchestration layer smell.

why_correct: B solves the actual problem — accidental drift from missing shared context — without eliminating the legitimate exception case. The CLAUDE.md entry establishes a team contract that makes the override intentional rather than accidental, which is exactly what Claude's settings layer is designed to support: defaults that apply when no override is given, plus documented conditions under which overrides are appropriate. This is the principle of least surprise with an escape hatch: the settings.json default handles the 90% case automatically, and the documentation ensures any deviation is deliberate and repeatable rather than a product of each engineer guessing at what 'default' means.

distractors.A: Hardcoding eliminates the legitimate edge-case workflow the senior engineers rely on — the stem explicitly says temperatures of 0.6–0.7 surface nuanced intent that strict extraction misses. A is correct when there is genuinely no valid override scenario, but that context doesn't apply here. Locking the parameter solves consistency at the cost of capability, which is the wrong tradeoff when a documented exception pattern already exists in production.
distractors.C: The failure mode here is treating enforcement as a substitute for documentation. Setting a default without a CLAUDE.md entry means the team still lacks a shared contract — engineers who need higher temperatures have no guidance on when that's appropriate vs. accidental, and the 'discover it via CLI help' assumption doesn't encode the team's judgment about when overrides are legitimate. In production this reproduces the original problem: intentional and accidental overrides are still indistinguishable.
distractors.D: Post-processing validation catches malformed output but doesn't address the upstream variance in what the model produces — a temperature=0.8 run generating loosely structured prose still fails the schema check and gets rejected, creating silent data loss rather than consistent extraction. This is the 'fix the symptom downstream' anti-pattern; it looks rigorous but increases pipeline fragility without resolving the root cause.

mental_model: When the root cause is missing shared context rather than a missing constraint, the correct fix is always documentation-plus-default, not lockdown. Reserve hardcoding for cases where no valid override exists; when a legitimate exception pattern is present, encode the decision rule in CLAUDE.md so overrides are intentional and auditable.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary design pattern in multi-agent and agentic loop contexts: you want defaults that are safe without operator intervention, but you preserve intentional escalation paths — same principle, different layer of the stack.

why_correct: The team lead's authority is scoped to CLAUDE.md and settings.json — so the governance mechanism must live there. Setting a default in settings.json establishes the baseline behavior for callers who don't override, while the CLAUDE.md rationale converts an implicit convention into an explicit, reasoned standard that engineers can calibrate against. This is the documentation-as-governance pattern: when you can't enforce at the infrastructure layer, you reduce deviation by eliminating the ambiguity that causes it. The 0.2–0.4 range with rationale is doing real work — it gives engineers a defensible boundary and the 'why,' which is exactly what was missing.

distractors.B: Few-shot examples shift output style through in-context learning, but they don't constrain sampling behavior — at temperature=0.8 the model still samples from a broader distribution, so you still get variance in commitment language and verbosity. Grounding via examples is the right tool when the problem is knowledge gaps or style drift; it's the wrong tool when the problem is stochastic variance in generation. The failure mode is false confidence: your examples look like they're working in spot-checks but the tail-risk outputs persist at volume.
distractors.C: C is architecturally correct in a world where the team lead has platform authority — infrastructure enforcement is strictly stronger than documentation. But the stem explicitly rules that out: platform team owns the codebase, and filing a request doesn't establish governance now. This is the near-miss: C is the right long-term answer, wrong immediate answer. In production, waiting on a platform request leaves the inconsistency window open indefinitely.
distractors.D: CLAUDE.md is a guidance artifact, not an enforcement mechanism — it has no technical ability to prohibit SDK parameter overrides. Framing a documentation entry as a prohibition creates a false sense of control. The failure mode is compliance theater: the policy exists, engineers violate it without consequence, and when a bad resolution ships, it's unclear whether the process failed or was never enforceable to begin with.

mental_model: When governance authority is scoped to documentation and config, your job is to eliminate ambiguity rather than enforce constraints. Default-plus-rationale in the appropriate artifact (settings.json + CLAUDE.md) is always the right move — it sets the baseline and gives engineers the 'why' that prevents well-intentioned drift. Reserve infrastructure enforcement requests for when documentation provably fails, not as a substitute for writing it down first.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary domain: when you can't harden a parameter at the API or infrastructure layer, you're managing risk through operator-layer conventions — same principle as using system prompt instructions when you can't modify the model itself.
